text: The dynamic restructuring of Copper (Cu) electrodes under electrochemical conditions is thought to be a fundamental driver of its unique catalytic activity toward the $CO_2$ reduction reaction ($CO_2$RR). Specifically, surface roughening—characterized by the formation of atomic vacancies and adatoms—is a critical activation phenomenon, yet the precise atomistic nature of these motifs under realistic adsorbate coverages remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the chemical space of Cu surface restructuring under mixed CO and H coverage using a combination of grand canonical density functional theory (GCDFT) and global optimization techniques. By sampling tens of thousands of unique states, we construct a potential-dependent grand canonical ensemble of the Cu(100), (111), and (711) interfaces. We observe significant vertical displacement of surface Cu atoms within a specific regime of intermediate CO and H coverage. Because this regime is kinetically controlled rather than thermodynamically favored, we developed a quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo simulation to track the non-equilibrium evolution of the electrode during a cathodic scan. Our simulations reveal that the system becomes trapped in metastable states featuring elevated Cu-CO species. Chemical bonding analysis indicates that these species are stabilized by the joint influence of atop CO ligands and surrounding H atoms, which "lubricate" the surface by weakening Cu-Cu metallic bonds. Furthermore, molecular dynamics and free energy calculations identify a novel wave-like propagation mechanism for Cu-CO diffusion and subsequent dimerization. These findings provide essential atomistic insights into the roughening and clustering of Cu catalysts, offering a methodological framework to study complex, non-equilibrium interfaces in electrocatalysis.
